content.text: Training data is the second axis of the scaling laws alongside model parameters. The Kaplan et al. (2020) laws showed that loss decreases as a power law in token count, and that for a fixed compute budget, the optimal strategy is to scale data and parameters together. The Hoffmann et al. (2022) "Chinchilla" paper refined this: a model trained on roughly 20 tokens per parameter reaches the compute-optimal frontier. Under-training large models on too little data is wasteful — a 70B model trained on 1T tokens is suboptimal; at 1.4T tokens (20× parameters) it is compute-optimal.
The Chinchilla Scaling Law
The Chinchilla paper fit a loss function over (N, D) — parameter count and token count — to determine the compute-optimal allocation for a given FLOPs budget
C ≈ 6ND

attrs.latex: N_{\text{opt}} \propto C^{0.5}, \quad D_{\text{opt}} \propto C^{0.5} \quad \Rightarrow \quad D_{\text{opt}} \approx 20 \cdot N_{\text{opt}}

content.text: This predicts that compute should be split roughly equally between parameters and data. Earlier models like GPT-3 (175B parameters, 300B tokens ≈ 1.7 tokens/param) were significantly under-trained by this criterion. Chinchilla (70B parameters, 1.4T tokens = 20 tokens/param) achieved better benchmark performance despite being 2.5× smaller than GPT-3.
Computing Optimal Token Count

def chinchilla_optimal(
    compute_flops: float | None = None,
    n_params: float | None = None,
    tokens: float | None = None,
    flops_per_token_per_param: float = 6.0,
) -> dict:
    """
    Given any two of (compute, parameters, tokens), compute the third
    and the optimal split according to Chinchilla scaling.

    flops_per_token_per_param: ~6 for a standard transformer forward+backward pass.
    """
    if compute_flops is not None and n_params is not None:
        tokens = compute_flops / (flops_per_token_per_param * n_params)
        return {"tokens": tokens, "tokens_per_param": tokens / n_params,
                "compute_flops": compute_flops, "n_params": n_params}
    elif compute_flops is not None and tokens is not None:
        n_params = compute_flops / (flops_per_token_per_param * tokens)
        return {"n_params": n_params, "tokens_per_param": tokens / n_params,
                "compute_flops": compute_flops, "tokens": tokens}
    elif n_params is not None and tokens is not None:
        compute_flops = flops_per_token_per_param * n_params * tokens
        return {"compute_flops": compute_flops, "tokens_per_param": tokens / n_params,
                "n_params": n_params, "tokens": tokens}
    else:
        raise ValueError("Provide exactly two of: compute_flops, n_params, tokens")


# Optimal data given a fixed model size
for n_b in [1, 7, 13, 70, 405]:   # billions of parameters
    n = n_b * 1e9
    optimal_tokens = 20 * n        # Chinchilla: 20 tokens per parameter
    result = chinchilla_optimal(n_params=n, tokens=optimal_tokens)
    print(f"{n_b:>4}B params → {optimal_tokens/1e9:.0f}B tokens  "
          f"(compute = {result['compute_flops']:.2e} FLOPs)")

#    1B params →  20B tokens  (compute = 1.20e+11 FLOPs)
#    7B params → 140B tokens  (compute = 8.40e+12 FLOPs)
#   13B params → 260B tokens  (compute = 1.56e+13 FLOPs)
#   70B params → 1400B tokens (compute = 8.40e+14 FLOPs)
#  405B params → 8100B tokens (compute = 4.86e+15 FLOPs)

# Real models vs Chinchilla-optimal
print("\nReal models vs Chinchilla-optimal:")
models = [
    # name,          n_params_B, tokens_B
    ("GPT-3",        175,   300),
    ("Chinchilla",    70,  1400),
    ("LLaMA 1 65B",  65,  1400),
    ("LLaMA 3 8B",    8, 15000),   # ~1875 tokens/param — deliberately over-trained
    ("LLaMA 3 70B",  70, 15000),   # ~214 tokens/param
]
for name, n_b, d_b in models:
    ratio = d_b / n_b
    status = "over-trained" if ratio > 30 else ("under-trained" if ratio < 15 else "near-optimal")
    print(f"  {name:<18} {n_b:>4}B params, {d_b:>5}B tokens, {ratio:.0f} tok/param — {status}")
Data Quality vs Quantity

Raw token count is not the only metric. The composition and quality of the training corpus matter significantly. Models like LLaMA 3 deliberately exceed the Chinchilla-optimal token count (15T tokens for a 70B model = 214 tokens/param) because they prioritise the resulting
inference-time efficiency
: an over-trained smaller model beats a Chinchilla-optimal larger model at the same inference cost. This is the "inference-compute-optimal" regime.
Data factor
Effect on model quality
Common practice
Deduplication
Removing near-duplicates reduces memorisation and improves generalisation
MinHash LSH or exact n-gram dedup
Domain mix
More code → better coding; more books → better reasoning
Domain weights tuned on downstream evals
Quality filtering
Removing low-quality web pages improves all benchmarks
Perplexity filter, n-gram overlap with reference corpus
Multilingual coverage
More tokens in a language → better generation in that language
Oversample low-resource languages to balance
Epoch repetition
Repeating data beyond 1 epoch degrades generalization; <4× repetition usually acceptable
Avoid with large enough unique datasets
Estimating Dataset Size

import tiktoken

enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")   # GPT-4 / LLaMA tokenizer (approximate)

def estimate_tokens(text: str) -> int:
    return len(enc.encode(text))

# Typical token densities for common sources
SOURCES = {
    "Web page (average)": 500,        # tokens
    "News article": 800,
    "Book (average, 80K words)": 110_000,
    "ArXiv paper": 8_000,
    "GitHub file (Python, average)": 400,
    "Wikipedia article": 1_500,
}

print("Token budget for a 7B Chinchilla-optimal run: 140B tokens")
print("That's roughly:")
for source, toks in SOURCES.items():
    count = 140_000_000_000 / toks
    print(f"  {count:>15,.0f}  {source}")

# Measure actual density on your data
sample_text = """The transformer architecture revolutionised natural language processing.
    Self-attention allows every token to attend to every other token in the sequence."""
print(f"\nSample text: {estimate_tokens(sample_text)} tokens for {len(sample_text.split())} words")
print(f"Words-per-token ratio: {len(sample_text.split()) / estimate_tokens(sample_text):.2f}")
# Typical English: ~0.75 words per token (1.33 tokens per word)

The Chinchilla result shifted the field from "bigger model = better" to "right-sized model on right-sized data". But the story keeps evolving: LLaMA 3's decision to train the 8B model on 15T tokens (1875 tokens/param, far beyond Chinchilla-optimal) showed that over-training a smaller model is often the right choice when serving cost matters more than training cost. The practical takeaway is that data quantity and quality are both first-class levers — not afterthoughts to be filled with whatever web crawl is available.
